verses.text: And again the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he moved David against them to say, Go, number Israel and Judah. For the king said to Joab the captain of the host, which was with him, Go now through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan even to Beersheba, and number ye the people, that I may know the number of the people. Guidance: The final chapter of 2 Samuel opens with one of the most theologically complex sentences in the Old Testament. 'The anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he moved David against them to say, Go, number Israel.' The reason for the LORD's anger is not specified — the narrative simply records it. Then the LORD 'moved' (vayyaset — incited, stirred up) David to take a census. The parallel passage in 1 Chronicles 21:1 attributes the inciting to Satan; this passage attributes it to the LORD. The two are not contradictory; the Old Testament regularly speaks of the LORD's sovereign permission of acts that proximate agents commit, with the LORD as ultimate cause and the lesser power (Satan, or human pride) as immediate cause. David's command: 'Go now through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan even to Beersheba, and number ye the people.' Why is taking a census sinful? The text does not say directly. Several factors are likely. (1) Mosaic law required a half-shekel ransom per head when the people were counted (Exod 30:12-16) — perhaps David did not collect it, treating the people as his possession rather than the LORD's. (2) The motive is military pride — David wants to 'know the number' as a measure of his own power, not for genuine administrative purpose. (3) The census represents trust in human resources rather than in the LORD who gives victory. The chapter is about to teach us that when a king of Israel counts his people for the wrong reason, the LORD will reduce the number by His own hand.

And Joab said unto the king, Now the LORD thy God add unto the people, how many soever they be, an hundredfold, and that the eyes of my lord the king may see it: but why doth my lord the king delight in this thing? Notwithstanding the king's word prevailed against Joab, and against the captains of the host. And Joab and the captains of the host went out from the presence of the king, to number the people of Israel. Guidance: Joab — the morally compromised hatchet-man who has killed Abner and Amasa — here plays the unlikely role of moral check. 'Now the LORD thy God add unto the people, how many soever they be, an hundredfold' — Joab can wish blessing on the people; his objection is to the counting, not the people's existence. 'But why doth my lord the king delight in this thing?' The question is shrewd. The verb chaphetz — delight, take pleasure — exposes the king's motive. David is not counting for any practical purpose; he is counting because he wants to see the number. Even Joab sees through it. The chapter is recording an interesting moral inversion: Joab, who has done worse than David in some respects (cold-blooded murder), is the one who tries to stop David here. The Bible is honest about its characters. None of them are morally one-piece; the same Joab who murdered Abner can also rebuke David's vainglory. 'Notwithstanding the king's word prevailed against Joab.' David overruled his commander; the census proceeded. The chapter is teaching us that authority is a dangerous thing — kings can override good counsel and proceed into sin precisely because no one can stop them. The same dynamic that produced Bathsheba (the king's word prevailed) produces the census.

And they passed over Jordan, and pitched in Aroer, on the right side of the city that lieth in the midst of the river of Gad, and toward Jazer: Then they came to Gilead, and to the land of Tahtimhodshi; and they came to Danjaan, and about to Zidon, And came to the strong hold of Tyre, and to all the cities of the Hivites, and of the Canaanites: and they went out to the south of Judah, even to Beersheba. So when they had gone through all the land, they came to Jerusalem at the end of nine months and twenty days. And Joab gave up the sum of the number of the people unto the king: and there were in Israel eight hundred thousand valiant men that drew the sword; and the men of Judah were five hundred thousand men. Guidance: The census tour. Joab and his officers spend nine months and twenty days circling the kingdom — east across the Jordan (Aroer, the Gad-river region, Jazer), north through Gilead and Dan to the Phoenician cities (Tyre, Zidon), then south through the Hivite/Canaanite enclaves and down to Beersheba. The route covers all the territory the LORD has given David. Final tally: 800,000 men of Israel + 500,000 men of Judah = 1,300,000 sword-bearing fighting men. (1 Chr 21:5-6 has slightly different numbers — 1,100,000 + 470,000 — and notes that Joab omitted Levi and Benjamin from the count because he found the task abhorrent.) The numbers are enormous; some scholars argue the Hebrew eleph here means 'clan' or 'unit' rather than a literal '1000,' which would adjust the totals significantly downward. Whatever the exact figure, the chapter is showing us David's army-roster — the kingdom's military potential. And exactly that potential is what David has sinned in counting. The size of his forces was supposed to be incidental to the LORD's victories; David has made it central. The chapter is about to demonstrate that a kingdom's true strength is never its head-count.

And David's heart smote him after that he had numbered the people. And David said unto the LORD, I have sinned greatly in that I have done: and now, I beseech thee, O LORD, take away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly. Guidance: Conscience arrives. 'David's heart smote him' — the Hebrew lev — heart-mind — strikes him. The verb is the same used for an attacking blow; conscience hits him like a fist. The chapter is showing us that the moral sense, when it finally activates, does not deliver gentle suggestions; it strikes. David's confession is unguarded: 'I have sinned greatly in that I have done.' No excuses; no rationalization; no blame-shifting. Then the petition: 'Take away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly.' He calls his action what it is — folly (siklah). The Hebrew word marks not just moral wrong but stupidity, foolishness. David recognizes that what looked like wisdom (knowing his military strength) was in fact folly (failing to recognize that strength comes from the LORD). The chapter is preserving an authentic biblical pattern of repentance: conscience strikes; the king confesses without qualification; the king pleads for the removal of guilt. This is the same David who confessed Bathsheba in chapter 12 and who wrote Psalm 51. He has not become incapable of repentance; even at the end of his reign, when his moral failures pile up, he still owns them. The chapter is teaching us that the man after God's own heart is not the man who never sins but the man who, when struck by conscience, repents.

For when David was up in the morning, the word of the LORD came unto the prophet Gad, David's seer, saying, Go and say unto David, Thus saith the LORD, I offer thee three things; choose thee one of them, that I may do it unto thee. So Gad came to David, and told him, and said unto him, Shall seven years of famine come unto thee in thy land? or wilt thou flee three months before thine enemies, while they pursue thee? or that there be three days' pestilence in thy land? now advise, and see what answer I shall return to him that sent me. Guidance: Gad the prophet — David's seer — arrives the next morning with the LORD's reply. The reply is striking: the LORD offers David three choices of punishment. The fact that he is offered a choice is itself an act of mercy; sinners normally do not get to select their consequences. The three options: (1) Seven years of famine. (1 Chr 21:12 has 'three years,' likely the original reading; the consequence-discipline is calibrated to land-scale — drought wipes out grain stores and animals, the kingdom shrinks through starvation. (2) Three months of military rout, pursued by enemies. The kingdom shrinks through battle-deaths and refugee flight. (3) Three days of pestilence. The kingdom shrinks through plague. Three options of different time-scales but parallel results: the LORD will reduce the very head-count David sinned in counting. The chapter is preserving the symmetry: David counted heads in pride; the LORD will lower the count to teach the lesson. The choice is real — Gad presents it as such, asking 'now advise, and see what answer I shall return to him that sent me.' David's selection will be his most theologically revealing act in the chapter.

And David said unto Gad, I am in a great strait: let us fall now into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man. Guidance: David's choice, and one of the great theological sentences in the Old Testament. 'I am in a great strait' (tzar li me'od — I am in great distress). The choice is agony; whichever way he picks, his people will suffer. Then the principle by which he chooses: 'Let us fall now into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man.' David elects the option that lies directly in the LORD's hand (plague) over the options mediated through human agency (famine works through nature/economy; enemy-pursuit works through human armies). His reason: 'his mercies are great' (ki rabim rachamav — for many are His mercies, His compassions). David trusts that direct contact with the LORD, even in judgment, will be less ruinous than indirect contact through human malice or impersonal disaster. This is one of the most generous theological statements ever made about God. David is saying: when the LORD strikes, He strikes with restraint informed by compassion. Humans, when they strike, strike with whatever moral configuration they happen to have. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's judgment is never separated from His mercy; the same hand that punishes is the hand that compassionates. The lesson is one believers throughout history have clung to when discipline came their way: better to be in the hands of the merciful God than in the hands of merciless circumstance.

So the LORD sent a pestilence upon Israel from the morning even to the time appointed: and there died of the people from Dan even to Beersheba seventy thousand men. And when the angel stretched out his hand upon Jerusalem to destroy it, the LORD repented him of the evil, and said to the angel that destroyed the people, It is enough: stay now thine hand. And the angel of the LORD was by the threshingplace of Araunah the Jebusite. And David spake unto the LORD when he saw the angel that smote the people, and said, Lo, I have sinned, and I have done wickedly: but these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, be against me, and against my father's house. Guidance: The plague descends. 70,000 die — a catastrophic number, especially read against the 1,300,000 of the census; nearly 5% of the army-strength erased. The pestilence runs across the same geography that David's census officers had traced — 'from Dan even to Beersheba.' The same span that registered the king's pride registers the LORD's discipline. Then the climactic scene at Jerusalem. 'The angel stretched out his hand upon Jerusalem to destroy it.' The destroying angel is poised over the holy city itself. 'The LORD repented him of the evil, and said to the angel that destroyed the people, It is enough.' The verb nicham — the LORD relented, was grieved into stopping. The chapter is showing us divine restraint at the very edge of catastrophe. 'It is enough' (rav) — a single word of mercy halts the angel. 'The angel of the LORD was by the threshingplace of Araunah the Jebusite.' The angel stopped at one specific spot — the threshing floor on the hill above the city. David's response: 'Lo, I have sinned, and I have done wickedly: but these sheep, what have they done?' One of the most pastoral sentences ever spoken by a king. David sees the dead and recognizes that the people did not choose his sin. They are sheep; he is the shepherd; the discipline has fallen on the wrong heads. 'Let thine hand, I pray thee, be against me, and against my father's house.' David offers himself in substitution. The shepherd-king takes responsibility for what his folly has cost his flock. The pattern is messianic: the shepherd dies for the sheep. The chapter is the seed-text of one of the deepest motifs in Scripture — leader-substitution for the led, ultimately fulfilled at Calvary.

And Gad came that day to David, and said unto him, Go up, rear an altar unto the LORD in the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite. And David, according to the saying of Gad, went up as the LORD commanded. Guidance: Gad returns with the LORD's direction. The spot where the destroying angel stopped — Araunah's threshing floor — is to be the site of an altar of atonement. The instruction is brief and specific. The threshing floor is on the hill above Jerusalem — the ridge that will become known as Mount Moriah, the same hill on which Abraham bound Isaac (Gen 22), and the future site of Solomon's temple (1 Chr 22:1; 2 Chr 3:1). The chapter is laying the foundation, literally, of one of the most theologically loaded locations in human history. The progression is unbroken: the place where Abraham almost slew his son becomes the place where David's plague-angel was stilled becomes the place where the temple's altar will stand becomes (in messianic-Christian reading) the larger geography on which the cross will be lifted. The narrative editor of 2 Samuel knows what he is doing in placing this episode at the very end of the book. The kingdom David built is not the goal; the temple-site David purchases is the goal. The end of 2 Samuel is, in effect, the beginning of the temple. 'David… went up as the LORD commanded.' Obedience without delay; the king moves immediately to obey the prophetic word.

And Gad came that day to David, and said unto him, Go up, rear an altar unto the LORD in the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite. And David, according to the saying of Gad, went up as the LORD commanded. Guidance: Gad returns with the LORD's direction. The spot where the destroying angel stopped — Araunah's threshing floor — is to be the site of an altar of atonement. The instruction is brief and specific. The threshing floor is on the hill above Jerusalem — the ridge that will become known as Mount Moriah, the same hill on which Abraham bound Isaac (Gen 22), and the future site of Solomon's temple (1 Chr 22:1; 2 Chr 3:1). The chapter is laying the foundation, literally, of one of the most theologically loaded locations in human history. The progression is unbroken: the place where Abraham almost slew his son becomes the place where David's plague-angel was stilled becomes the place where the temple's altar will stand becomes (in messianic-Christian reading) the larger geography on which the cross will be lifted. The narrative editor of 2 Samuel knows what he is doing in placing this episode at the very end of the book. The kingdom David built is not the goal; the temple-site David purchases is the goal. The end of 2 Samuel is, in effect, the beginning of the temple. 'David… went up as the LORD commanded.' Obedience without delay; the king moves immediately to obey the prophetic word.

And Araunah looked, and saw the king and his servants coming on toward him: and Araunah went out, and bowed himself before the king on his face upon the ground. And Araunah said, Wherefore is my lord the king come to his servant? And David said, To buy the threshingfloor of thee, to build an altar unto the LORD, that the plague may be stayed from the people. And Araunah said unto David, Let my lord the king take and offer up what seemeth good unto him: behold, here be oxen for burnt sacrifice, and threshing instruments and other instruments of the oxen for wood. All these things did Araunah, as a king, give unto the king. And Araunah said unto the king, The LORD thy God accept thee. Guidance: The threshing-floor scene. Araunah (1 Chr 21:18 has 'Ornan') is a Jebusite — one of the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem (which David had captured back in ch 5). He is presumably wealthy (he owns a threshing floor on the hill above the city) and possibly retained some standing in the city. He sees the king approaching and bows. David explains his purpose: 'To buy the threshingfloor of thee, to build an altar unto the LORD, that the plague may be stayed from the people.' Note the explicitly substitutionary frame — the altar's purpose is to stay the plague. Araunah's response is generous: he offers everything free. The threshing floor itself, the oxen for burnt offerings, even the wooden threshing implements for kindling wood. 'All these things did Araunah, as a king, give unto the king.' The Hebrew is touching — Araunah gives 'like a king' (ha-kol natan Aravnah ha-melek la-melek — 'all these things Araunah the king gave to the king'). The Jebusite shows royal-scale hospitality. 'The LORD thy God accept thee' — even more touching, the Jebusite blesses the Israelite king with reference to the LORD whose altar is being built. The chapter is preserving a moment of unexpected friendship across ethnic lines. The plague has come; the threshing-floor owner offers freely; the LORD's altar will rise on a site of cross-ethnic generosity.

And the king said unto Araunah, Nay; but I will surely buy it of thee at a price: neither will I offer burnt offerings unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing. So David bought the threshingfloor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver. And David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So the LORD was intreated for the land, and the plague was stayed from Israel. Guidance: The closing scene of 2 Samuel — and one of the most quoted lines in the book. 'Nay; but I will surely buy it of thee at a price: neither will I offer burnt offerings unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing.' David refuses the free gift. He insists on paying. His reasoning is theologically vital: an offering that costs the offerer nothing is not really an offering. Sacrifice that requires no sacrifice is not sacrifice. The chapter is teaching us one of the foundational principles of worship: the LORD wants what costs us, not what costs us nothing. The free token has no real value to the giver and therefore no real meaning before the LORD. David pays. 'So David bought the threshingfloor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.' (1 Chr 21:25 records 600 shekels of gold for the larger site; the 50 shekels here is likely for the threshing floor and the oxen only.) The number 50 is symbolically resonant — it is the Jubilee number, the year of restoration. David builds the altar; offers burnt offerings and peace offerings; 'and the LORD was intreated for the land, and the plague was stayed from Israel.' The verb va-ye'ater — 'was intreated, was prevailed upon' — closes the book. The LORD responds to the costly offering by ending the discipline. The book of 2 Samuel — which began with David's grief over Saul and Jonathan, which traced his rise to throne and his catastrophic fall and his agonized recovery and his ambivalent restoration — ends with David at an altar on a hill above Jerusalem, offering sacrifice that costs him, and watching the LORD stay His hand from His people. The chapter, and the book, ends as a story of restoration. The temple Solomon will build will rise on this exact spot. The Messiah Christians worship is the ultimate Davidic Son who, on a hill outside the same Jerusalem, offered the supreme costly sacrifice and stilled the plague of sin on His people forever. 2 Samuel ends with the threshing floor; the New Testament begins by recalling that, of David's seed, came Christ.
